title: Automatic Sensitivity Control in the Sensing Chain
text: The Auklet RC-5 platform's sensing chain relies on automatic sensitivity control, ASC, to track each patient's intrinsic cardiac signal rather than depending on a fixed detection threshold programmed once at implant. After every sensed or paced event, the firmware measures the peak amplitude of that event and sets the next sensing threshold as a percentage of the measured value, letting the threshold decay toward a programmable floor across a short refractory window before the next expected beat. This decaying-threshold approach follows amplitude changes that occur naturally as scar tissue forms around the lead tip in the weeks after implant, without requiring a clinic visit to reprogram sensitivity by hand. A threshold set too aggressively risks oversensing far-field skeletal muscle noise as an intrinsic beat; one set too conservatively risks undersensing and an inappropriately withheld pacing pulse. Auklet's engineering team adopted the decaying-threshold model after the platform's earliest fixed-threshold prototypes proved brittle across the wide amplitude range seen across real patient populations.

title: Lead Impedance Monitoring Parameters
text: The RC-5 firmware measures pacing lead impedance automatically once every 12 hours as of firmware 3.2 and later, using a low-amplitude subthreshold pulse that does not capture the heart. Normal impedance for the RC-5's standard bipolar lead family falls between 300 and 1200 ohms. A reading above 2000 ohms sustained across three consecutive checks is classified as a probable lead fracture, while a reading below 200 ohms sustained across two consecutive checks is classified as a probable insulation breach. Each measurement is stored in a rolling 30-day trend buffer, and a change greater than 20 percent from the 7-day trailing average triggers an elevated-priority telemetry flag even when the absolute value remains within the nominal 300-1200 ohm band. Firmware 2.0 and later retain up to 180 days of impedance history for clinic review.

title: Sensing Behavior Across Firmware Generations
text:
Sensing behavior has tightened measurably across four RC-5 firmware generations, as summarized below.

| Firmware (year) | ASC decay window | Impedance check interval | Documented oversensing rate |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1.0 (2018) | 250 ms | 24 h | 0.41% |
| 2.0 (2020) | 180 ms | 24 h | 0.33% |
| 3.2 (2024) | 140 ms | 12 h | 0.09% |
| 4.3 (2026) | 140 ms | 12 h | 0.07% |

The largest single drop in oversensing rate followed the 3.2 patch rather than either of the two prior major releases, reflecting a targeted algorithm fix rather than gradual tuning.

title: Sensing Threshold Decay and Noise-Floor Margin
text: RC-5's automatic sensitivity control decays the sensing threshold as V_th(t) = V_peak * e^(-t / tau) + V_floor, where V_peak is the amplitude of the most recently sensed event, tau is a programmable decay constant, and V_floor is a minimum sensitivity floor. Firmware 3.2 requires V_th(t) >= 2 * V_noise at every point in the refractory window, a two-times noise-floor margin chosen after field data showed the 1.5x margin used in firmware 1.0 and 2.0 allowed myopotential noise bursts to cross threshold during vigorous arm motion. The same decay schedule must also satisfy V_th(t_next) <= 0.5 * V_expected by the time the next physiologic beat is due, so the threshold decays enough to remain sensitive. Tau is tuned per chamber, since atrial and ventricular signal morphology decay at measurably different rates.

title: Implant Commissioning and Sensing Calibration Procedure
text:
During RC-5 implant, the commissioning team follows a fixed calibration sequence before closing the pocket.
1. Measure intrinsic P-wave and R-wave amplitude on the just-placed lead using the programmer's real-time electrogram view.
2. Set the initial sensing threshold to 50 percent of the measured intrinsic amplitude, subject to the firmware's programmable floor.
3. Confirm pacing lead impedance falls within the 300-1200 ohm nominal range before proceeding.
4. Program the rate-adaptive sensor blend and confirm the accelerometer responds to gentle manual tapping on the device pocket.
5. Verify the Auklet Home Monitor registers the implant and completes a test telemetry transmission.
6. Record all baseline values in the patient's device card before discharge.
A commissioning session that fails step three is not considered complete until the lead is repositioned and impedance is rechecked.

title: Blending Two Sensors for Rate-Adaptive Pacing
text: Rate-adaptive pacing on the RC-5 platform blends two independent activity sensors rather than relying on either alone. An accelerometer mounted on the pulse generator's header responds within roughly one second to physical motion, giving a fast but physiologically crude estimate of exertion that can be fooled by vibration unrelated to activity, such as riding in a car over rough pavement. A minute-ventilation sensor, which measures transthoracic impedance changes driven by breathing depth and rate, responds more slowly, typically over ten to twenty seconds, but tracks true metabolic demand far more closely, including exertion that produces little body motion, such as climbing stairs while carrying a load. The firmware combines both signals into a single blended rate-response output, weighting the fast accelerometer signal more heavily during the first seconds of a detected change and shifting weight toward the minute-ventilation signal as the activity period continues, so early responsiveness does not come at the cost of physiologic accuracy over a sustained effort.

title: Home Monitor Telemetry Timing and Alert Latency
text: The Auklet Home Monitor transmits a full diagnostic summary from each implanted RC-5 device every night between 1:00 and 4:00 a.m. local time over a cellular gateway, with a typical payload of 40 to 60 kilobytes per transmission. Red-flag events, including suspected lead fracture, sustained oversensing, and battery voltage below the elective-replacement threshold, trigger an immediate out-of-cycle transmission rather than waiting for the nightly window. Across the installed base as of the most recent quarterly report, median alert-to-clinician latency for red-flag events was 14 minutes, and 97.4 percent of red-flag alerts reached a clinician within 60 minutes. Routine, non-urgent nightly transmissions succeed on the first attempt 98.9 percent of the time, with the remainder retried automatically up to three times before being logged as a missed transmission.

title: German Research on Adaptive Sensing Thresholds
text: Adaptive sensing-threshold design did not originate with Auklet; the RC-5 team's own bibliography credits earlier German work on noise suppression in implantable pulse generators. Brandt and Wexler's 2019 study, Rauschunterdrückung in implantierbaren Herzschrittmachern: ein Vergleich adaptiver Schwellenwertverfahren, compared several adaptive-threshold techniques and directly informed the decay-window redesign shipped in RC-5 firmware 3.0. Their central conclusion was stated plainly: Ein fest programmierter Schwellenwert kann die natürliche Schwankung der Signalamplitude nicht ausreichend abbilden — a fixed, permanently programmed threshold cannot adequately capture the natural variation in signal amplitude across a real patient population. Auklet's hazard-analysis documentation cites the paper directly alongside the IEC 60601 series when justifying the platform's move away from fixed-threshold sensing used in earlier prototype hardware.

title: FAQ: How Often Is Lead Impedance Checked?
text: Q: How often does the RC-5 check pacing lead impedance? A: The RC-5 firmware measures pacing lead impedance automatically once every 24 hours as of firmware 3.2 and later, using a low-amplitude subthreshold pulse that does not capture the heart. This question comes up often from clinic staff comparing the RC-5 to older Auklet platforms that checked impedance only at in-office follow-up visits. Between automatic checks, the device continues normal sensing and pacing without interruption, and the impedance measurement itself is imperceptible to the patient. Clinics can also request an on-demand impedance check through the programmer at any office visit, independent of the automatic schedule.

title: The SafeCore-C3 Certification Pathway
text: Auklet submits every RC-5 firmware release through the SafeCore-C3 certification pathway before it reaches an implantable device, a process the company built specifically around IEC 62304's Class C designation for software whose failure could contribute directly to death or serious injury. A SafeCore-C3 review requires a documented hazard analysis for every changed code path, full statement and branch coverage from the automated test suite, and independent review by an engineer who did not write the changed code. The pathway also maps each release against ISO 14708-2's requirements for active implantable cardiac devices, since firmware behavior alone cannot satisfy that standard without corresponding hardware verification. A release cannot proceed to limited clinical release until every open hazard identified during analysis is either mitigated in the release itself or formally accepted as residual risk by Auklet's safety review board, whose sign-off is recorded against the specific firmware build number.

title: Certification Milestones by Firmware Release
text: RC-5 firmware 1.0 completed SafeCore-C3 certification in June 2018, ahead of the platform's first human implants later that year. Firmware 2.0, adding the dual-sensor rate-adaptive blend, was certified in April 2020; firmware 3.0, which overhauled remote telemetry, followed in January 2022. The corrective release, firmware 3.2, completed an expedited SafeCore-C3 re-certification in March 2024 covering only the changed sensing-classification code path, rather than a full-scope review. Firmware 4.0 completed a full-scope re-certification in November 2024, and the current release, firmware 4.3, was certified in March 2026. Across all six certifications, the hazard-analysis log has grown from 340 identified hazards for firmware 1.0 to 610 for firmware 4.3, with static-analysis tooling now covering 99.2 percent of firmware source lines.

title: Battery Longevity by Pacing Burden and Sensor Mode
text:
Projected battery longevity depends heavily on pacing burden and which rate-response sensor mode is active, as the reference model below shows.

| Pacing burden | Accelerometer only | Blended (default) | Minute ventilation only |
|---|---|---|---|
| 10% | 13.2 yr | 12.8 yr | 11.9 yr |
| 50% | 11.6 yr | 11.1 yr | 10.0 yr |
| 100% | 10.9 yr | 10.5 yr | 9.2 yr |

Minute-ventilation sensing alone costs roughly a full year of projected longevity compared to the default blend, which is why the blend, not the more physiologically accurate single sensor, ships as the factory default.

title: Projected Battery Longevity Model
text: RC-5's projected longevity model estimates remaining battery life as L = C / (I_base + b * I_pace + d * I_sense), where C is rated battery capacity in amp-hours, I_base is quiescent current draw, b is pacing burden expressed as a fraction between 0 and 1, I_pace is per-beat pacing current at the programmed output, and I_sense is the current cost of the active rate-response sensor mode. Minute-ventilation sensing carries a higher I_sense than accelerometer-only sensing because it requires periodically driving a small excitation current across the thoracic impedance path, which is why d, the sensing-mode weight, dominates the longevity gap between sensor modes at low pacing burden. Auklet chose this additive linear model over the multiplicative model used in the earlier RC-3 platform because field data showed pacing current and sensing current do not meaningfully interact.

title: Japanese Research on Continuous Impedance Trending
text: Trend-based fracture detection, now central to RC-5's impedance monitoring, draws on Japanese engineering work Auklet's hazard documentation cites directly. Yamada et al.'s 2017 paper, 植込み型心臓ペースメーカーにおけるリード抵抗の連続監視, argued for continuous rather than visit-only impedance measurement. As the authors put it, 山田らの2017年の論文「植込み型心臓ペースメーカーにおけるリード抵抗の連続監視」は、断線の早期検出におけるトレンド解析の有効性を示した — Yamada et al.'s 2017 paper demonstrated the effectiveness of trend analysis for early detection of lead fracture. That finding shaped the RC-5 decision to store a rolling impedance trend buffer on-device rather than relying solely on threshold crossings, since a fracture often produces a gradual impedance drift before any single reading crosses an absolute limit.

title: Field Safety Response Checklist for Implanting Centers
text:
Implanting centers follow a standard response sequence whenever a patient's RC-5 generates an elevated-impedance or oversensing alert.
1. Confirm the alert against the device's own stored trend data during the next available telemetry session, rather than acting on a single flagged reading alone.
2. Bring the patient in for an in-office interrogation within five business days for a red-flag alert, or at the next routine visit for a lower-priority flag.
3. Reassess pacing and sensing thresholds directly against the fresh in-office measurement.
4. Check the current firmware build against Auklet's published corrective-action list for that alert type.
5. Escalate to Auklet's clinical support line if the in-office measurement confirms the automatic alert.
6. Document the resolution in the patient's device record regardless of outcome.
Centers that skip step one most often reschedule patients unnecessarily for alerts that later trend data shows were transient.

title: Global Deployment and Firmware Update Statistics
text: As of the first quarter of 2026, the Auklet RC-5 platform has been implanted in more than 84,000 patients across 31 countries, with roughly 22,000 of those implants completed in the twelve months ending March 2026. The installed base runs a mix of firmware versions: 3 percent still run 3.0 or earlier, 41 percent run 3.2, and 56 percent run 4.0 or 4.3. Over-the-air firmware updates, introduced with firmware 3.0's telemetry overhaul, have been delivered to 61,000 devices to date, with a 99.6 percent successful-application rate on the first attempt. The remaining 0.4 percent required a follow-up in-clinic update, most often because the Home Monitor lost its connection mid-transmission.

title: From Fixed Thresholds to the Blended Sensor Model: RC-5's Design Path
text: Auklet Medical began the RC-5 program in 2016 as a successor to an earlier, fixed-threshold pacemaker line whose sensing behavior required frequent in-clinic reprogramming. Firmware 1.0, certified and first implanted in 2018, introduced the decaying-threshold ASC model but paced using a single accelerometer for rate response, a simple approach that struggled to distinguish genuine exertion from vibration. Firmware 2.0, in 2020, added the minute-ventilation sensor and the blended rate-response model still in use today. Firmware 3.0, in 2022, replaced periodic in-clinic telemetry downloads with the always-on Home Monitor architecture, a change the team now describes as the single most consequential shift in how RC-5 patients are followed clinically. The 3.2 corrective release in 2024, discussed in detail elsewhere in this reference, tightened the ASC noise-floor margin after a field-detected oversensing pattern. Firmware 4.0 and 4.3, in 2024 and 2026, focused mainly on battery-model refinement and expanded remote-monitoring analytics rather than further sensing changes.

title: Incident Postmortem: February 2024 Oversensing Cluster
text:
Beginning on February 11, 2024, Auklet's clinical monitoring team identified a cluster of Home Monitor alerts describing intermittent pauses in ventricular pacing across eleven RC-5 patients spread across three implanting centers, all running firmware 3.1. Each affected patient shared a common lead configuration: a specific bipolar ventricular lead model paired with a generator implanted within the prior four months, a combination present in roughly 6,400 of the installed base at the time. The alerts themselves did not indicate a lead fracture; impedance readings for all eleven patients remained within the normal 300-1200 ohm range throughout the affected period.

Engineering's investigation, completed March 2, 2024, traced the root cause to firmware 3.1's noise-classification logic. During the brief high-impedance transient that occurs naturally in the first months after implant, as fibrous tissue forms around a newly placed lead, skeletal-muscle myopotential noise occasionally crossed the decaying sensing threshold and was misclassified as an intrinsic ventricular beat. The firmware, behaving correctly given that misclassification, inhibited the next scheduled pacing pulse, producing the pauses reported by the affected patients' monitors. No patient required emergency intervention, though two reported transient lightheadedness that resolved without treatment.

Auklet released firmware 3.2 on March 19, 2024, widening the noise-floor margin used during the early post-implant transient specifically, from 1.5 times to 2 times the measured noise floor, a margin that reverts automatically after 120 days once the transient period reliably ends. The corrective code path was carried through an expedited SafeCore-C3 review rather than the full-scope process, since the change was narrowly scoped to the classification logic identified as the root cause.

Auklet filed a field safety notice with the FDA and its EU notified body on March 25, 2024, and notified all implanting centers with affected lead-generator combinations within five business days. Devices already deployed with Home Monitor connectivity received the 3.2 update automatically over the following six weeks; the small remaining population without reliable telemetry connectivity was scheduled for in-clinic updates, completed by June 2024 for 98 percent of that group.

The postmortem's central finding was that the noise-floor margin had been validated primarily against steady-state impedance conditions rather than the early post-implant transient specifically, a gap the hazard analysis for firmware 1.0 through 3.0 had not explicitly tested. Auklet's hazard-analysis template was revised afterward to require dedicated transient-period test cases for any sensing-threshold change, a requirement now standard for every subsequent release including 4.0 and 4.3. Auklet also committed to publishing an annual summary of transient-period test coverage in its clinical newsletter, so implanting centers can track the change independently of any future release notes.

title: Glossary: Installed Base
text: Installed base: the cumulative count of Auklet RC-5 units actively implanted and in clinical use, as distinct from units manufactured or shipped. As of the first quarter of 2026, the Auklet RC-5 platform has been implanted in more than 94,000 patients across 31 countries, with roughly 22,000 of those implants completed in the twelve months ending March 2026. Auklet recalculates this count quarterly from Home Monitor registration records rather than shipment logs, since a shipped unit is not counted as part of the installed base until its first successful telemetry transmission. Units returned unimplanted, or explanted for reasons unrelated to device performance, are removed from the installed-base count in the following quarterly reconciliation. The installed-base figure appears in every quarterly clinical summary Auklet publishes to implanting centers.

title: Firmware Footprint and Real-Time Core Timing
text: The RC-5 generator runs its sensing and pacing firmware on a dual-core microcontroller clocked at 24 MHz for the real-time pacing core and 8 MHz for the telemetry and diagnostics core, a split chosen to keep the timing-critical pacing loop isolated from telemetry workload. Firmware 4.3 occupies 412 kilobytes of the device's 1 megabyte of non-volatile program storage, leaving headroom for over-the-air delta updates without a full reflash. The real-time pacing core's scheduling loop runs a fixed 2-millisecond cycle with worst-case execution time measured at 340 microseconds, leaving substantial timing margin. Total firmware source, including test harnesses, spans roughly 186,000 lines across the 4.3 release, up from 94,000 lines at firmware 1.0.
